YopouLab Exploratory Survey — April/May 2026

Yopougon, Abidjan — Aggregated descriptive report

Author

YopouLab consortium

Published

June 8, 2026

YopouLab
Loading interactive charts…
Contents

    About this survey

    This interactive report presents aggregated descriptive statistics from a cross-sectional household survey conducted in Yopougon Millionnaire-Extension, Abidjan, Côte d’Ivoire (April–May 2026). Only anonymised aggregate data are included; no individual-level records are published.

    Building on stakeholder consultations conducted in May 2025, this is the first quantitative survey of YopouLab — a transdisciplinary urban public health research initiative led by the Swiss Tropical and Public Health Institute (Swiss TPH) and the Centre Suisse de Recherches Scientifiques en Côte d’Ivoire (CSRS).

    Goal and objectives

    The survey pursues a twofold aim:

    1. Primary objective — population and housing data for HDSS design. Generate fine-scale, neighbourhood-level data on living conditions, household composition, and population dynamics to assess the feasibility and strategic design of the first urban Health and Demographic Surveillance System (HDSS) in Côte d’Ivoire.

    2. Secondary objective — intra-urban health disparities. Determine highly localized urban health priorities as perceived by residents, while quantifying how different urban habitats in Yopougon relate to self-reported health and wellbeing outcomes. We focused on: mental health (depression, alcohol use, psychological flourishing), heat stress, age-friendly environments, and risk factors associated with mosquito-borne diseases.

    Study design and strata

    Households were recruited through a stratified two-stage cluster sampling design. The study area was stratified into three built-environment types derived from remote urban morphology analysis and in-situ verifications:

    Stratum Description
    Informal (red) Dense, irregular built fabric; limited urban infrastructure
    Hybrid (orange) Mixed built fabric; transitional urban form
    Formal (green) Structured built fabric; better urban infrastructure

    Within each cluster, households were systematically recruited by doorstep approach. The household head (or a designated adult resident equally responsible for the household) answered to a structured questionnaire administered by a trained surveyor. For individual-level health outcomes (perceived general health, mental health, heat stress, age-friendly environments), all consenting adult household members (18+) were eligible, but a maximum of 2 adults in each household answered to those individual health questions.

    Overview

    1495
    Total surveys
    563
    Informal
    438
    Hybrid
    494
    Formal
    Collection period: 2026-04-27 → 2026-05-23
    19 days (D1 → D19) · 32 clusters

    Household characteristics

    NoteVariable definitions
    • Household size: Total number of persons who usually sleep in the household (all ages). Collected via a household roster (one row per member).
    • % Single-person households: Proportion of households where only one person lives.
    • % HH with resident aged 50+: Proportion of households with at least one member aged 50 or above.
    • % HH with resident aged 60+: Proportion of households with at least one member aged 60 or above.
    • % HH with child under 1 year: Proportion of households with at least one infant aged 0 (not yet turned 1).
    • % HH with child under 5 years: Proportion of households with at least one child aged 0–4.
    • By stratum & total
    • By cluster
    • Map

    Housing conditions

    NoteVariable definitions
    • Water source: The household’s main source of drinking/domestic water. Categories: SODECI piped water (inside the house/compound or external), mineral/bottled/sachet water, water bowser (camion-citerne), borehole, protected well, other.
    • Water availability: Whether the household ran out of water in the past month (binary: yes / no).
    • Toilet type: The type of sanitation facility used by the household. Categories include flush toilet, improved latrine, traditional pit latrine, open defecation or other.
    • Wall material: The main material of the outer walls of the household’s main dwelling. Categories: concrete/brick (béton/brique), earthen/compressed earth (banco/pisé), sheet metal (tôle), wood (bois), straw (paille).
    • % Basic sanitation (composite): Household has access to an improved sanitation facility (flush toilet or improved latrine), not shared with other households.
    • % Basic hygiene (composite): Household has a functional handwashing station with water and soap available at the time of the survey.
    • % Overcrowded (>2/room) (composite): The number of persons per room exceeds 2 (total residents / total rooms).
    • % Closed water storage (composite): Among households that store drinking water, those that use a closed/covered container (prevents contamination).
    • By stratum & total
    • By cluster
    • Map

    Mobility

    NoteVariable definitions
    • Trips out of Abidjan: How often the household head spends at least one full day outside Abidjan (for any reason). Categories: ≥ weekly, ≥ monthly, ≥ yearly, Never. A separate question clarifies overnight stays (≥ 2 days). Percentage base: all households that declared at least 1 trip per year (excluding DK/refused).
    • Duration at current address: How long the household head has lived at the current address, captured in continuous minutes (subsequently categorised as < 1 year, 1–5 years, 5–10 years, > 10 years, always lived here).
    • Previous place of residence: Among those who moved to the current address, where did they previously live? Categories: same neighbourhood, other Yopougon neighbourhood, other Abidjan commune, other Ivorian city/village, another country. Percentage base: households where the head reported having moved to the current address.
    • By stratum & total
    • By cluster
    • Map
    • Residential mobility

    Healthcare access

    NoteVariable definitions
    • Health insurance (any card): % of all roster individuals (all ages, n = 7,286) who hold any health insurance card, regardless of validity. This is an individual-level proportion — numerator: count of individuals with a card; denominator: total roster individuals — not a mean of household percentages.
    • Health insurance (valid card): % of all roster individuals (n = 7,286) who hold a currently valid health insurance card at the time of the survey. Same denominator as above.
    • Healthcare consultation (past 12 months): Whether the respondent (individual level) consulted a health provider in the past 12 months (yes/no). Percentage base: all individual respondents in the sub-sample.
    • Place of last consultation: Among those who consulted a health provider, where did the last consultation take place? Categories include: public primary facility, public hospital, private clinic, pharmacy, traditional healer, other. Percentage base: individuals who reported having consulted.
    • Financial barrier: Whether the respondent reported that cost was the main reason for not consulting a health provider when needed in the past 12 months (yes/no). Percentage base: all individual respondents in the sub-sample.
    • Insurance
    • Healthcare seeking
    • Financial barriers

    Demographics

    NoteVariable definitions
    • Age group: Age of each household member collected via the household roster, grouped into 5-year bands (0–4, 5–9, … 70–74, 75+). Expressed as percentage of all roster members. Source: complete household roster (n = 7,286 individuals).
    • Sex: Biological sex of each roster member (male / female). The % female is the proportion of all household members who are female.
    • Age pyramid: Mirrors male (left) and female (right) age distributions; bars show each age group as % of the full roster.
    • Age pyramid
    • Age — by stratum & total
    • Sex ratio
    • Map

    Mental health & wellbeing

    NoteVariable definitions
    • PHQ-9 (Patient Health Questionnaire-9): A validated 9-item depression screening tool. Respondents rate how often they experienced each symptom in the past 2 weeks (0 = not at all, 3 = nearly every day). Total score: 0–27. Severity bands: 0–4 minimal, 5–7 mild, 8–9 moderate, 10–11 moderately severe, 12–14 moderate-to-severe, 15+ severe. Administered to adult household members (18+) in the sub-sample (1-2 participants in each household). Percentage base: all sub-sample respondents (n varies slightly by item completion).
    • PHQ-9 moderate-to-severe (≥ 8): Binary indicator derived from PHQ-9 total score ≥ 8, grouping all moderate, moderately severe, and severe categories.
    • Self-reported health status: One-item global health question (“How would you rate your overall health?”), 5 categories: Very poor, Poor, Fair, Good, Very good. Administered to all adult sub-sample respondents.
    • AUDIT-C (Alcohol Use Disorders Identification Test — Consumption): 3-item alcohol screening tool assessing frequency of drinking, quantity per occasion, and frequency of heavy episodic drinking. Score: 0–12. Higher scores indicate higher risk of hazardous drinking. Administered to all adult sub-sample respondents.
    • Flourishing Scale: A scale of psychological and social well-being, with each item rated 1–5 (1 = strongly disagree, 5 = strongly agree). Higher scores indicate greater flourishing. Mean score shown per item and as overall mean. Administered to all adult sub-sample respondents (1-2 participants in each household).
    • PHQ-9 — by stratum & total
    • PHQ-9 — by cluster
    • PHQ-9 — moderate-to-severe (≥ 8)
    • Health status — by stratum & total
    • AUDIT-C
    • Flourishing — radar
    • Map

    Age-Friendly Environments

    Administered to respondents aged 50 and over (0-1 participant in each household). Scale: 1 = Strongly agree → 5 = Strongly disagree.

    NoteVariable definitions
    • AFE Scale items: Statements adapted from the WHO Age-Friendly Environments framework, covering domains of outdoor spaces, transportation, housing, social participation, civic engagement, communication, and community support. Each item rated on a 5-point scale: 1 = Strongly agree (most favourable) → 5 = Strongly disagree (least favourable). Lower mean scores indicate more age-friendly conditions. Administered to all sub-sample respondents aged 50 and over.
    • Self-reported diseases (50+): Respondents aged 50+ were asked whether they had ever been diagnosed with specific chronic conditions (hypertension, diabetes, vision problems, hearing problems, musculoskeletal conditions, etc.). Expressed as % of respondents aged 50+.
    • Self-reported medication (currently): Whether the respondent is currently taking any prescribed medication regularly (yes/no). % of respondents aged 50+.
    • Fallen indoors / Fallen outdoors (past year): Whether the respondent experienced a fall inside or outside the home in the past 12 months (yes/no). % of respondents aged 50+.
    • Radar — by stratum & total
    • Bar — by stratum & total
    • By cluster
    • Health conditions (50+)

    Urban health priorities

    Respondents selected up to 3 perceived health priorities in their neighbourhood over the past 12 months.

    NoteVariable definitions
    • Urban health priorities: Respondents (individual level, sub-sample) were asked to select up to 3 health issues they perceived as most important in their neighbourhood over the past 12 months. Options include: heat at home, lack of cleanliness / outdoor sanitation, road conditions, lack of accessible transport, mosquitoes / vector-borne disease, air pollution, lack of green spaces, noise, and other. Percentages are computed as % of respondents who selected each option (multi-select: denominator = all respondents; values sum to > 100%).
    • By stratum & total
    • By cluster

    Heat stress

    All questions administered to the household head.

    NoteVariable definitions
    • Heat priority: Whether very high temperatures are a concern for the respondent or their household. Scale: 1 = Yes, a lot → 4 = Not at all.
    • Daytime heat comfort: How comfortable the respondent feels when it is very hot during the day. Scale: 1 = Not comfortable at all → 5 = Very comfortable.
    • Sleep comfort in heat: How comfortable the respondent’s sleep is when it is very hot at night. Scale: 1 = Very bad, cannot sleep → 4 = Comfortable, sleeps well.
    • Hot days impact health: Whether the respondent thinks very hot days can impact their health (yes/no). Denominator: all valid responses.
    • Health actually affected: Whether the respondent or someone in the household is actually affected by heat. Denominator: those who said hot days can impact health.
    • Daily activities harder: Whether daily activities are more difficult to carry out on very hot days (yes/no). Denominator: all valid responses.
    • Takes protective action: Whether the respondent takes any action to protect themselves or their household from heat (yes/no).
    • Adaptation actions: Actions taken to protect from heat (multi-select). Denominator: households that take at least one protective action.
    • Who is affected: Age groups affected by heat in the household (multi-select). Denominator: households where health is affected by heat.
    • Heat-related symptoms: Health effects reported when it is very hot (multi-select). Denominator: households where health is affected by heat.
    • Sought care due to heat: Whether the respondent or household member sought care specifically because of heat (yes/no). Denominator: all valid responses.
    • Place of care seeking: Where care was sought (multi-select). Denominator: households that sought care for heat.
    • Reason for care seeking: Reason for seeking care (multi-select). Denominator: households that sought care for heat.
    • Perceives climate/weather change: Whether the respondent thinks something has changed regarding weather/climate in recent years (yes/no).
    • Perceived changes: What changes the respondent perceived (multi-select). Denominator: those who perceive a change.
    • Heard of climate change: Whether the respondent has heard the term “climate change” (yes/no).
    • Perception & comfort
    • Health impact & care seeking
    • Adaptation & climate
    • Map

    Report generated from pre-aggregated and pseudonymised data only. Raw survey data stored locally and never published. Wilson 95% CIs throughout. Household- and individual-level tabs contain only analysis variables with cluster-level geographic resolution; no GPS, names, or submission identifiers are present. Cluster polygons from field KML files (all 32 clusters, including combined days 16–17 and 18–19). YopouLab consortium / Swiss TPH & CSRS — 2026.
